macro_segments.title: The Meaning of 'When an Egg Comes Out, It Is Complete' and Its Application to Mekach Ta'ut (Erroneous Sales)

micro_segments.title: Rav's Statement on Egg Completion and the Gemara's Initial Inquiry into Its Practical Implications
The Case of Fertilized vs. Unfertilized Eggs in the Marketplace: Why Purchasing Laid Eggs Implies Intent to Hatch Chicks
The Case of Eggs from Male Chickens: Distinguishing Between Quality Preference and Purpose of Hatching

macro_segments.title: The Definition of Egg Completion: Majority Emerged vs. Fully Emerged, and Rabbi Yochanan's Ruling on Erev Yom Tov

micro_segments.title: Competing Interpretations: Majority Emerged (Rubah) vs. Fully Emerged (Kulah) as the Standard for Completion
Rabbi Yochanan's Halacha: An Egg Majority-Emerged Before Yom Tov Becomes Permitted on Yom Tov

macro_segments.title: Eggs Within a Slaughtered Hen: When Are They Considered Meat for Basar Be-Chalav and Tumah?

micro_segments.title: Complete Eggs Without Shells: Permitted with Milk; Rabbi Yaakov's Stringency When Intertwined with Sinews
The Braita on Eating from a Non-Shechted Bird: Distinguishing Which Parts Cause Tumah
Determining Authorship: Is the Braita's Position That of Rabbi Yaakov or Not?
Abaye's Distinction: Rabbi Yaakov May Be Strict Only for Basar Be-Chalav, Not for Tumah

macro_segments.title: When Animals Give Birth: The Relationship Between Mating Habits (Diurnal, Nocturnal, Both) and Birth Timing

micro_segments.title: The General Principle: Species Mate and Give Birth at the Same Times of Day
Application to Hens: They Only Give Birth During Daytime Because They Only Mate During Day

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Egg Found in the Coop: Determining Whether It Was Laid on Yom Tov or Erev Yom Tov Using the Principle That Hens Only Lay Fertilized Eggs at Night When a Rooster Is Present

micro_segments.title: Rav Mari's Teaching: If No Egg Was Found Before Nightfall But One Appears in the Morning, It Must Have Been Laid During Daytime
Addressing the Objection: The Egg Could Have Been Laid at Night If Unfertilized; But a Rooster's Presence Ensures Fertilization
Ravina's Tradition: When a Rooster Is Nearby, the Hen Will Not Lay Unfertilized Eggs
Determining Proximity: A Rooster's Voice Audible During Daytime Establishes That the Hen Will Not Lay Unfertilized Eggs
Rav Mari's Practical Ruling: An Egg Is Permitted Even If Laid Overnight When a Rooster Is Within Sixty Houses
Physical Obstacles to Roosters: Rivers Block Crossing, But Roosters Will Cross Bridges; Thin Planks Prevent Crossing, Yet a Rooster Did Cross One
Resolution: When Egg Timing Is Uncertain, Leniency Applies Because We Assume It Was Laid Before Yom Tov (Safek Derabanan Le-Kula)
Tosafot's Caveat: The Leniency of Safek Derabanan May Not Apply to Objects That Will Become Permitted Later (Davar She-Yesh Lo Matirin)

macro_segments.title: Chametz and Se'or on Pesach: The Debate Between Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel on Minimum Quantities and Whether Both Are Treated Identically

micro_segments.title: Beit Shammai's Position: Se'or (Sourdough) Transgresses at the Size of an Olive; Chametz at the Size of a Pressed Date
Beit Shammai's Reasoning: Se'or's Powerful Leavening Power Warrants a Smaller Prohibition Threshold
Beit Hillel's Position: Both Chametz and Se'or Transgress at the Same Size—an Olive
Beit Hillel's Reasoning: Each Has Its Own Stringency; Both Need Explicit Mention to Prevent Erroneous Leniency
The Gemara's Analysis: The Dispute Concerns Ownership (Biyur), Not Eating; All Agree Eating Transgresses at an Olive
Conceptual Implications of Beit Shammai's Position: Ownership as Distinct from Eating Concerns

macro_segments.title: Covering the Blood of Wild Animals and Birds Slaughtered on Yom Tov: When Is Preparation Permitted, and Debate Within Beit Shammai on Pre-existing Dirt

micro_segments.title: Initial Mishnah Ambiguity: Is the Question Lechatchila (Prospectively) or Di-eved (Retroactively)?
Rava's Interpretation: The Dispute Is Lechatchila; Beit Shammai Permits, Beit Hillel Forbids
Rav Yosef's Alternative Formulation: A Slightly Different Ordering of Instructions (Dig, Shecht, Cover vs. Shecht, Dig, Cover)
Abaye's Investigation: The Difference Between Formulations Reflects a Dispute About Rav Zeira's Requirement for Loose Dirt Below the Blood
Rav Yosef's Clarification: Both He and Rava Hold of Rav Zeira, but They Debate Whether Beit Shammai Requires Pre-existing Loose Dirt
Rava's Stringency: Prohibit Digging Lechatchila Unless Loose Dirt Already Exists, to Prevent Abandonment of the Slaughter
Rav Yosef's Leniency: Permit Digging Even Without Pre-existing Dirt, Accepting the Risk of Abandonment in Order to Preserve the Joy of Yom Tov
